lessons.title: for Loop Basics
lessons.summary: Writing loops that repeat a specific number of times

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The basic syntax of the [[for loop]] (initialization; condition; update)
- How to use loop [[variables]] (counters)
- Repeating a process a specified number of times

Loops give your programs the power of repetition.

contents.title: Writing a for Loop
contents.body: A for loop is written in the form: for (initialization; condition; update) { body }.
contents.note: i is a "counter variable" that counts the number of iterations. By convention, i, j, and k are used.

contents.title: Understanding the Three Parts of a for Loop
contents.body:
A for loop consists of three parts.

for (①Initialization; ②Condition; ③Update)

| Part | Role | Example |
|---|---|---|
| ①Initialization | Prepare the loop variable | int i = 0 |
| ②Condition | Set the condition for repeating | i < 10 |
| ③Update | Execute after each iteration | i++ |

Execution order: ① → ② → body → ③ → ② → body → ③ → ... → loop ends when ② is false
contents.note: i++ means "increment the value of i by 1". It is the same as i = i + 1.

contents.title: Execution Order of a for Loop
contents.body:
| Step | Description |
|---|---|
| ① Initialization (int i = 0) | Runs only once at the start |
| ② Condition check (i < 5) | If true, execute the body |
| ③ Body execution | The code inside { } |
| ④ Update (i++) | Advance the counter |
| ⑤ Return to ② | Repeat until the condition is false |

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- The for loop is ideal when the number of iterations is known
- By convention, the counter starts at 0
- The loop ends when the condition becomes false
- i++ is executed once per iteration

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: The loop starts at i=1 and iterates while i<=3. It prints 1 2 3. print does not add a newline.

quizzes.question: Which of the following lists the three elements of a for loop in the correct order?
quizzes.explanation: A for loop follows the order: for (initialization; condition; update). They are separated by semicolons.

quizzes.question: How many times does the following loop iterate?
quizzes.explanation: The loop iterates 5 times for i=0, 2, 4, 6, 8. Since i increments by 2 with i+=2, the output is 0, 2, 4, 6, 8.

lessons.title: while Loop
lessons.summary: Repeating as long as a condition is true

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The syntax of the [[while loop]] and when to use it
- The difference from the do-while loop
- **Infinite loops** and how to avoid them

Use a while loop when the number of iterations is not known in advance.

contents.title: Writing a while Loop
contents.body: A while loop is written in the form: while (condition) { body }.
contents.note: If the condition never changes inside the loop, it becomes an **infinite loop**. Always include code that moves toward the exit condition.

contents.title: Infinite Loop and break Pattern
contents.body:
A common real-world pattern is to intentionally create an **infinite loop** and exit with break when a condition is met.
By writing while(true), you can flexibly control the exit condition inside the loop body.
contents.note: while(true) is a standard expression for intentionally creating an **infinite loop**. Always make sure to include a break condition.

contents.title: Watch Out for Infinite Loops
contents.body:
If the condition of a while loop stays true forever, the program will never end.

Common mistakes:
- Forgetting to update the [[variable]] used in the condition
- Using the wrong comparison [[operator]] (e.g., using <= instead of <)

If you accidentally create an **infinite loop**, you can force-stop it with the IDE's stop button or Ctrl+C.
contents.note: An intentional **infinite loop** while(true) is sometimes used in combination with a break statement.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- A while loop repeats as long as the condition is true
- Best suited when the number of iterations is not known in advance
- Beware of **infinite loops**: always include an update that moves toward the exit condition
- while(true) + **break** is a standard pattern for intentional infinite loops
- Choosing between [[for loop]] and while: use for when the count is known, while when it is not

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: n goes from 10→7→4→1→-2. When n>0 becomes false (at -2), the loop ends and -2 is printed.

quizzes.question: What causes an infinite loop in a while loop?
quizzes.explanation: If there is no code inside the loop to change the condition, the condition stays true forever, resulting in an infinite loop.

quizzes.question: Which scenario is best suited for a while loop?
quizzes.explanation: A while loop is ideal when the number of iterations is not known in advance. Waiting for user input is a typical example.

choices.text: Processing all elements of an array

quizzes.question: Complete the for loop that calculates the sum from 1 to 5.
quizzes.explanation: A for loop consists of three elements: (initialization; condition; update). Use <= to include 5 in the range.

lessons.title: do-while Loop
lessons.summary: A loop that always executes at least once

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The syntax of the **enhanced for loop** (for-each loop)
- How to iterate through all elements of an [[array]] or collection
- When to use a regular [[for loop]] vs. an enhanced for loop

The [[enhanced for loop]] makes your code simpler.

contents.title: Writing a do-while Loop
contents.body: A do-while loop is written in the form: do { body } while (condition); Note the semicolon at the end!
contents.note: The key difference from a [[while loop]] is the timing of the condition check. while = pre-check, do-while = post-check.

contents.title: "Executes at Least Once" Is the Key Point of do-while
contents.body:
The biggest difference between a while loop and a do-while loop is the timing of the condition check.

| Loop | Condition Check | Minimum Executions |
|---|---|---|
| while | Checks first (pre-check) | 0 (not executed if condition is false) |
| do-while | Checks after (post-check) | 1 (always executes at least once) |

Use a do-while loop in situations where you want to "execute once first, then check the condition" (e.g., displaying a menu, accepting input).

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- do-while "always executes at least once"
- The condition check is at the end of the loop (post-check)
- Don't forget the semicolon after while (condition);
- Useful for scenarios like repeated menu display

contents.title: Choosing Between for / while / do-while
contents.body:
Use the three loop statements according to the situation.

| Loop | Characteristics | Use Case |
|------|------|--------|
| for | Number of iterations is clear | "Repeat 10 times", "All elements of an array" |
| while | Number unknown, pre-check | "Until a condition is met" |
| do-while | Number unknown, post-check | "Must execute at least once" |
contents.note: When in doubt, use a for loop. It is the most commonly used loop statement in Java.

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: A do-while loop executes the body first, so even though x is 100, it is printed once. Since the condition x<10 is false, the loop ends after one iteration.

quizzes.question: What is the biggest difference between a do-while loop and a while loop?
quizzes.explanation: A do-while loop always executes the body at least once, even if the condition is false (post-check). A while loop checks the condition first (pre-check), so it may not execute at all.

choices.text: It always executes at least once
The types of variables that can be used are different

quizzes.question: Why does the following do-while loop cause a compilation error?
quizzes.explanation: A semicolon (;) is required at the end of a do-while loop. It must be written as } while (true);.

quizzes.question: Select all valid loop constructs in Java.
quizzes.explanation: Java has four loop constructs: for, while, do-while, and enhanced for (also known as for-each). The keyword foreach does not exist in Java.

lessons.title: Loop Control
lessons.summary: Controlling loops with break and continue

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How to exit a loop early with the break statement
- How to skip to the next iteration with the continue statement
- How to control nested loops with labeled break/continue

These are important techniques for controlling the flow of loops.

contents.title: break and continue
contents.body: These are two keywords that control the flow of a loop.
contents.note: break is also used in [[switch statements]]. If you omit break inside a case in a switch statement, execution "falls through" to the next case.

contents.title: Difference Between break and continue
contents.body:
These are easy to confuse, so make sure to understand the distinction.

| Keyword | Behavior | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| break | Terminates the loop entirely | "I'm done!" |
| continue | Skips the current iteration and moves to the next | "Skip this one, next!" |

break moves "outside" the loop, while continue returns to the "top" of the loop.
contents.note: Overusing these can make code harder to read, so use them only when necessary.

contents.title: Labeled break and continue
contents.body: Use labels to control the outer loop in nested loops.
contents.note: Any identifier can be used as a label name (e.g., outer, loop1). By convention, names that describe the role of the loop, such as outer: or row:, are used.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
| Keyword | Behavior |
|---|---|
| break | Exits the entire loop |
| continue | Skips the current iteration and moves to the next |
| Labeled break | Exits all the way out of nested loops |
| Labeled continue | Skips to the next iteration of the outer loop |
- Without a label, break/continue applies to the innermost loop
- Label syntax: place labelName: immediately before the for/[[while loop]]

quizzes.question: The statement that skips the remaining code in the current iteration and moves to the next iteration is ____.
quizzes.explanation: The continue statement skips the remaining code in the current iteration and moves to the next iteration. The break statement terminates the loop entirely.

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: When j==1, continue outer moves to the next iteration of the outer i loop. Only j=0 is executed for each i, resulting in the output: 00 10 20.

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: i=0 → printed. i=1 → skipped by continue. i=2 → printed. i=3 → loop exits with break. The result is 0 2.

quizzes.question: Arrange the components of a do-while loop in the correct order.
quizzes.explanation: A do-while loop executes the do block first, then checks the condition with while. It always executes at least once.

lessons.title: Nested Loops
lessons.summary: Writing a loop inside another loop

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The structure of nested loops (double loops)
- The execution order of double loops
- Practical use cases such as multiplication tables and coordinate processing

By placing a loop inside another loop, you can express complex repetitions.

contents.title: Double Loops
contents.body: For each iteration of the outer loop, the inner loop runs from start to finish.
contents.note: When the outer loop has i=1, the inner loop runs through all of j=1, 2, 3. Then i becomes 2, and j=1, 2, 3 runs again.

contents.title: Watch the Execution Count of Double Loops
contents.body:
If the outer loop runs N times and the inner loop runs M times, the inner body executes N × M times.

- Outer 5 times × Inner 10 times = 50 total executions
- Outer 100 times × Inner 100 times = 10,000 total executions

Double loops can cause a rapid increase in processing, so avoid unnecessary nesting.
contents.note: Triple or deeper nesting significantly reduces code readability. Consider splitting the logic into [[methods]].

contents.title: Triangle Pattern — Using the Outer Variable in the Inner Loop
contents.body: By varying the inner loop count based on the outer [[variable]], you can create patterns where the output count changes per row.
contents.note: Since the inner loop condition is j < i, the number of * increases as i increases. This is a common pattern that uses nested loops.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- For each iteration of the outer loop, the inner loop runs completely
- The total execution count is a product (outer × inner)
- Used for multiplication tables, coordinates, and tabular data processing
- Using the outer variable in the inner loop condition enables patterns like triangles
- Avoid nesting three or more levels deep, as it reduces readability

quizzes.question: How many times is * displayed in the following code?
quizzes.explanation: Outer 3 times × Inner 4 times = 12 times. Twelve * characters are displayed.

quizzes.question: How do you calculate the total number of executions in a double loop?
quizzes.explanation: In a double loop, the total number of executions is the outer loop count × the inner loop count.

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: Outer i=0: inner j=0 → prints "00", j=1 → break. Outer i=1: inner j=0 → prints "10", j=1 → break. The result is 00 10.
